status_notes: Initial research from GCP Persistent Disk documentation, 2026-04-04. Pass 1 (service docs) and partial Pass 2 (API). Needs Pass 3 (cross-reference) and Pass 5 (human review with live GCP environment).
notes: Block storage for Compute Engine. Security focus: encryption.

match_nodes.description: Encryption at rest for Persistent Disk volumes

data.description: All persistent disks are encrypted at rest automatically. Data is encrypted at the storage layer before being written to disk. Three key management options available.

options.name: Google-managed encryption keys (GMEK)
options.description: Default. Google manages keys with automatic rotation. No configuration required. No additional cost.

options.name: Customer-managed encryption keys (CMEK)
options.description: Keys managed in Cloud KMS. Customer controls key rotation, access, and lifecycle.

options.name: Customer-supplied encryption keys (CSEK)
options.description: Customer provides a raw or RSA-wrapped key with each request. Google does not store the key. Key loss means data loss.

default.note: All persistent disks encrypted by default with Google-managed keys. No opt-out possible.

data.vendor_recommendation: Use CMEK with Cloud KMS for regulatory requirements or key lifecycle control. GMEK is sufficient for most workloads. Avoid CSEK unless required — key loss means permanent data loss.

configure.note: CMEK must be specified at disk creation time. Cannot change encryption key after creation.

data.description: Snapshots inherit encryption from the source disk by default. GMEK-encrypted disks produce GMEK-encrypted snapshots. CMEK-encrypted disks produce snapshots encrypted with the same key, or a different CMEK can be specified. CSEK-encrypted disks require the original key to create a snapshot.
data.vendor_recommendation: Ensure snapshot encryption aligns with source disk encryption policy. Use CMEK for snapshots that need to be shared across projects with consistent key management.

match_nodes.description: Additional encryption for Confidential VM workloads

data.description: Confidential VMs provide hardware-based memory encryption using AMD SEV (Secure Encrypted Virtualization) or Intel TDX (Trust Domain Extensions). Disk data is protected by a VM-specific key that is generated and managed by the hardware security processor. Provides defense against cloud operator and physical attack vectors.

options.description: AMD Secure Encrypted Virtualization. Encrypts VM memory with per-VM key. Available on N2D and C2D machine types.
AMD SEV with Secure Nested Paging. Adds integrity protection. Stronger isolation guarantees.
Intel Trust Domain Extensions. Hardware-isolated VM execution environment with encrypted memory.

data.vendor_recommendation: Use Confidential VMs for workloads processing highly sensitive data where protection from the cloud operator and side-channel attacks is required.

audit.console: Compute Engine → VM instances → {vm} → Details → Confidential VM service

configure.note: Must be specified at VM creation time. Cannot be enabled on existing VMs.
